explanation_md: `a` is a named variable — an lvalue — so `a.show()` calls the `&`-qualified overload, printing `lval: hello`. `Label{"world"}` creates a temporary — an rvalue — so `.show()` calls the `&&`-qualified overload, printing `rval: world`.

prompt_md: A class has `int getData() const &` and `int getData() &&`. Which objects call which overload?

options.a: `const &` is called on non-const objects; `&&` is called on const objects.
options.b: `const &` is called on lvalue objects (named variables, including const); `&&` is called on rvalue objects (temporaries).
options.c: `&&` is called on lvalue objects; `const &` is called on rvalue objects.
options.d: Both overloads are called for every object; the compiler picks the last one defined.

explanation_md: Ref qualifiers dispatch based on value category, not `const`-ness. `const &` is called when the object is an lvalue (a named, addressable object — const or not). `&&` is called when the object is an rvalue (a temporary). The `const` in `const &` qualifies `this` as const within that overload, so both const and non-const lvalues call the `const &` overload.

prompt_md: You have a class with `const std::string& name() const &` returning a reference to a private member. Why might you add `std::string name() &&` alongside it?

options.a: To allow calling `name()` on non-const objects.
options.b: To prevent returning a dangling reference when `name()` is called on a temporary.
options.c: To make the function callable from a static context.
options.d: To avoid copying the string when the object is an lvalue.

explanation_md: When called on a temporary (rvalue), the object will be destroyed at the end of the expression. Returning `const std::string&` would point into destroyed memory — a dangling reference. The `&&`-qualified overload returns by value instead: the string is copied (or moved) out safely before the temporary dies. This is the canonical reason to pair a `const &` reference-returning getter with a `&&` value-returning getter.

options.a: `int get() &` is not valid syntax; the `&` must come before the return type.
options.b: You cannot have both an unqualified `get()` and a `&`-qualified `get()` — once you use a ref qualifier on one overload, all overloads of that function must be ref-qualified.
options.c: `int get() &` returns by value from an lvalue, which always causes a compile error.
options.d: The unqualified `get()` and `get() &` have identical signatures and cause an ambiguity error.

explanation_md: C++ does not allow mixing an unqualified overload with a ref-qualified overload for the same function — once any overload has a ref qualifier, all overloads must have one. The fix is to remove the unqualified `int get()` and keep only `int get() &`, or to add a `&&`-qualified overload and remove the unqualified one. Providing both `int get()` and `int get() &` creates an inconsistent set that the compiler rejects.
